attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman is being reviewed for ongoing symptoms of auditory hallucinations. Some 18 months ago her symptoms started when she began hearing voices through the ceiling at work. The voices were talking about private information about her love life that she feared others would hear. She also described having thoughts inserted into her mind that came from somewhere else and that others could tell what she was thinking. At that time she appealed to be reallocated to a different workstation, as far away from the office radio as possible because the voice from the radio had been communicating with her. She had been experiencing recurring thoughts of suicidal ideation in response to voices which told her to end her life before someone does it for her.

She was trialled on risperidone for two months, and then on quetiapine for a further two months, both medications reaching maximal doses. Both she and her family report her taking the medication as directed. However, her symptoms persist. She now appears dishevelled, severely malnourished, and underweight.  She is thought disordered and has a blunted affect while at the same time talking about being distressed about the voices. She denies any homicidal or violent intentions but does demonstrate suicidal ideation without any current specific intent.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate alternative to her current medication regime?
attributes.explanation:
This patient previously experienced first rank symptoms of schizophrenia such as thought insertion and thought broadcasting, alongside auditory hallucinations and referential delusions. Despite being treated with two different antipsychotics at maximum doses, her symptoms persisted beyond six weeks. This renders the problem a treatment-resistant schizophrenia, for which clozapine is indicated as first line treatment. Clozapine has also been found to be more therapeutic than other second generation atypical antipsychotics, for patients with high levels of persistent suicidality.

Aripiprazole is efficacious in treating non-treatment resistant schizophrenia, but is not indicated for treatment-resistant schizophrenia and is commonly associated with restlessness and akathisia as side effects.

Clozapine is indicated in treatment-resistant schizophrenia and a reduction in suicidal ideation and suicide is more pronounced in patients on clozapine than with second generation antipsychotics such as olanzapine, quetiapine and risperidone.

Sodium valproate is a mood stabiliser and not an antipsychotic agent and as such is not used as monotherapy in schizophrenia. It is more often used in Bipolar Disorder as maintenance therapy.

Haloperidol, a first generation antipsychotic, is indicated in non-treatment resistant schizophrenia and is more often associated with strong and disabling extra-pyramidal side effects and hence is not used as first line treatment.

Olanzapine is an atypical second generation antipsychotic (D2 antagonists). Generally, second generation antipsychotics are considered more ideal than first generation antipsychotics, due to their weaker D2 antagonist action, and thus, less severe side effects (particularly, extrapyramidal and anticholinergic side effects). However, there are other side effects that can be problematic. Some examples are: metabolic syndrome and dose-dependent weight gain are more common (olanzapine, quetiapine); strong dose-dependent EPSE and hyperprolactinemia are seen with risperidone; and weight gain and sedative effects from quetiapine. For these reasons second generation antipsychotics are not always ideal and individual patient responses need to be assessed.
learningPoints: Clozapine is indicated in severe treatment resistant schizophrenia with persistent suicidal ideation
First generation antipsychotics vs second generation antipsychotics
references: Lally J, Gaughran F, Timms P, Curran SR. Treatment-resistant schizophrenia: current insights on the pharmacogenomics of antipsychotics. Pharmgenomics Pers Med. 2016 Nov 7;9:117-129. doi: 10.2147/PGPM.S115741. PMID: 27853387; PMCID: PMC5106233. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27853387/

Lundblad, W., Azzam, P., Gopalan, P., Ross, C. and PharmD, 2015. Medical management of patients on clozapine: A guide for internists. Journal of Hospital Medicine, 10(8), pp.537-543. Available from: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0269881108093588

Haidary HA, Padhy RK. Clozapine. [Updated 2023 Nov 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK535399/
